About Me

I was once exactly where you are now.

I moved from India to France with nothing but a stack of documents, a lot of hope, and zero idea what to expect. The bureaucracy was confusing, the language was a challenge, and finding housing felt impossible.

Three years later, I have two Master's degrees, international work experience in France, and a genuine passion for making that journey easier for every student who comes after me.

I know what it feels like to be overwhelmed. That's why I offer the kind of personalised, honest guidance I wish I had from day one.

The Process

📄

🏠

Pre-Arrival Preparation

While still at home, we prepare your accommodation search, plan your first week, and make sure you know exactly what to bring.

✈
Step 05

Welcome to France

I receive you at the airport and accompany you to your accommodation. Your first day in France won't feel lonely or scary.

🎓
Step 06

Full Settlement Support

SIM card, bank account, OFII registration, CAF application, student card — I stay with you until you are fully settled.

FAQ

Questions students ask me most

Everything you're wondering about — answered honestly, from experience.

🏛  Visa & Campus France
What is Campus France and do I really need it?+
Campus France is the official French government platform that manages international student applications. Most Indian students applying to French universities must register on Campus France and attend an interview at the French consulate before getting a visa. It sounds intimidating, but with the right preparation it's very manageable — and I guide you through every step.
Not sure where you stand? Book a call →
How long does the visa process take?+
From the moment you start your Campus France dossier to receiving your visa typically takes 6–10 weeks, depending on the consulate and time of year. I always recommend starting at least 3 months before your intended travel date to avoid last-minute stress.
Want a personalised timeline? Book a call →
Can I get a student visa without knowing French?+
Yes — many programs in France are fully taught in English, especially at the Master's level. The Campus France interview is typically conducted in French, but for English-taught programs it's often a mix. I help you prepare for this interview so you know exactly what to expect, regardless of your French level.
What documents do I need for my visa application?+
The list includes your passport, university admission letter, proof of financial means, accommodation proof, academic transcripts, language certificates, and more. The exact requirements vary slightly by consulate. I provide a personalised document checklist and review everything before you submit — so nothing gets rejected for a missing paper.
Get your personalised checklist →
🏠  Housing & Accommodation
How do I find student accommodation in France?+
The main options are university residences (CROUS), private student residences, shared apartments (colocation), and homestays. CROUS is the most affordable but competitive — applications open early. I help you navigate all platforms, shortlist options based on your budget and city, and avoid the many scams that target international students.
Is it safe to book accommodation before arriving in France?+
It can be, but there are many rental scams targeting students who book remotely. I guide you on which platforms are trustworthy, what red flags to watch for, and how to secure accommodation safely. For your first few nights, I also recommend short-term options while you search on the ground.
How much does renting a room in France cost?+
It varies by city. In Paris, expect €600–€900/month for a studio or shared room. In cities like Lyon, Bordeaux, or Nantes, you can find options for €350–€600/month. CAF housing benefit can reduce your rent by €100–€200/month, which I help you apply for after arrival.
💸  Cost & Pricing
Why does the initial consultation only cost €3?+
I keep the first call affordable because I remember how uncertain it feels before you even know if someone can really help you. The €3 covers a genuine 30-minute consultation where we assess your situation together. If you need further support, we discuss the right package from there — no pressure, no hard sell.
How much should I budget for my first month in France?+
Realistically, plan for €1,200–€1,800 for your first month including rent, food, transport, a SIM card, and initial admin costs. After CAF kicks in and you settle into a routine, monthly expenses typically drop to €800–€1,200 depending on your lifestyle and city.
Are there any hidden costs I should know about?+
Yes — and most students are surprised by them. The CVEC student contribution (~€103/year), health insurance top-up, transport pass deposit, and the cost of apostille documents back home can add up quickly. I walk you through all of these in advance so nothing catches you off guard.
🏭  Life in France — Banking, SIM & Admin
How do I open a bank account in France as a student?+
Most traditional banks require a French address and proof of enrolment. Online banks like Boursorama or Wise are much easier to open quickly. I help you decide the best option for your situation and walk you through the process — I've done it myself and with several students.
What is OFII and what do I need to do?+
OFII (Office Français de l'Immigration et de l'Intégration) is the French immigration office. After arriving in France, you must validate your long-stay visa with OFII within 3 months — this is mandatory or your visa becomes invalid. The process involves a medical check and form submission. I make sure you do this on time and without confusion.
Can I work part-time while studying in France?+
Yes — student visa holders can work up to 964 hours per year (roughly 20 hours/week). You don't need a separate work permit. Many students work in retail, hospitality, or tutoring. I can point you toward resources for finding student jobs, including on-campus opportunities.
What is CAF and how do I apply?+
CAF is the French family allowance fund that provides housing benefit (APL) to eligible students. Depending on your rent and situation, you could receive €100–€250/month directly into your account. You apply online after arriving and having a French address and bank account. I guide you through the application — it can be confusing but the savings are worth it.
Need help with CAF? Book a call →
🎓  Studies & University Life
How do I choose the right university or school in France?+
France has public universities, grandes écoles, and private business/engineering schools. Each has different entry requirements, costs, and reputations. I help you shortlist programs based on your academic background, career goals, budget, and preferred city — so you apply to the right places, not just the most famous ones.
Is it hard to adapt to the French education system?+
It can be a shift — French academic culture values critical thinking, debate, and written analysis differently from the Indian system. Group projects, oral presentations, and independent research are common. I share practical tips on what to expect so you walk into your first lecture feeling prepared, not lost.
